attributes.stem:
A 65-year -old man presents with intermittent urinary incontinence three months after a radical prostatectomy performed for Gleason 4+4=8, T2bN0M0 carcinoma of the prostate. He experiences some leakage of urine when he coughs, sneezes or picks up heavy objects. Otherwise he had a good urinary stream and no other urinary symptoms. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. He takes perindopril and metformin.

Physical examination, including urinalysis, is unremarkable and he only has a small post-void volume in his bladder.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following management options is most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has stress urinary incontinence (SUI), characterised by small volumes of incontinence on coughing, sneezing or straining. Some degree of SUI is extremely common in the first few months after radical prostatectomy. Chronic severe SUI is rarer, occurring in <5% of men. First-line treatment for stress incontinence is conservative, with pelvic floor muscle training. This consists of maximal isometric contraction of pelvic floor muscles, held for 6-8 seconds and repeated as a set of 10 contractions 3 times per day. This is often taught to the patient by a continence physiotherapist. Patient's should be counselled that it could take months for their incontinence to improve and surgical intervention should be delayed until at least 6-12 months after prostatectomy. For patients with severe SUI an artificial urethral sphincter (AUS) is often performed, for those with less severe SUI a male urethral sling procedure may suffice.

Tamsulosin is a selective 𝛼-blocker that relaxes smooth muscle in the bladder neck and prostate. It is used in the treatment of bladder outlet obstruction (BOO) secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). Given this patient's recent radical prostatectomy bladder outlet obstruction secondary to BPH is impossible and so this is an inappropriate treatment. BOO can occur after prostatectomy but usually due to the development of bladder neck stenosis, a late complication which does not respond to an 𝛼-blocker.

Duloxetine has been shown to reduce symptoms of stress urinary incontinence and increase quality of life in females with SUI; however, in post-prostatectomy incontinence it should be reserved for more mild cases that are not responsive to conservative measures or moderate-severe cases where patient's decline or are not fit for further surgery.

Penile clamp can be used in the management of stress incontinence; however, it is reserved for cases that are unresponsive to conservative measures in patients who are too co-morbid to undergo a surgical procedure such as an AUS.

Although a mid-urethral sling is commonly used in women for stress incontinence, it is more rare for men to undergo surgery with insertion of a sling. Furthermore, this is not first line, and is reserved for cases of moderate severity that do not respond to pelvic floor exercises.
learningPoints: Conservative measures including pelvic floor exercises are first-line for post-prostatectomy urinary stress incontinence and should be trialled before other options such as medications or surgery especially in the immediate post-operative period.

Counselling patients carefully about expected post-operative changes and how long they might last is critical in managing expectations and patient satisfaction after surgery. Especially for a potentially socially embarrassing and debilitating condition such as urinary incontinence.
references: Campbell SE, et al: Conservative management for postprostatectomy urinary incontinence. Cochrane Database System Rev, Issue 1: CD001843, 2012.
Lucas MG, et al: Guidelines on urinary incontinence. European Association of Urology, 2014 (www.uroweb.org).
Jost WH, Marsalek P. Duloxetine in the treatment of stress urinary incontinence. Ther Clin Risk Manag. 2005 Dec;1(4):259-64. PMID: 18360568; PMCID: PMC1661641.
